7: A direct revelation: 'When the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire, inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus.' The parousia of Jesus is described using the vocabulary of YHWH's theophanic judgment in Isa 66 and Daniel — the same fire, angels, and judgment associated with YHWH's Day of the Lord are now associated with Jesus's return. The Christological identification is complete: the Day of the Lord is the Day of the Lord Jesus; the theophanic judge is Christ.
1: The salutation joins Paul, Silas, and Timothy as senders — a collegial apostolic fellowship that itself embodies the communal dimension of Christ. The address 'in God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ' places Father and Son in a single prepositional phrase, a syntactic coupling that implies co-equal divine status. The Thessalonian assembly exists 'in' both — constituted by their shared divine life.
2: 'Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.' The grace-peace benediction is characteristically Pauline, but its source — God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together — identifies Christ as the co-giver of divine grace. Grace in Paul's usage is the redemptive energy of the cross applied to believers; sourcing it from Christ and Father jointly signals Christ's divine agency in salvation.
3: 'Your faith is growing abundantly and the love of every one of you for one another is increasing.' Faith and love together describe the community's Christological formation: faith is the reception of Christ; love is the Christomorphic pattern of his self-giving reproduced in the community (Philippians 2:1–5). The growth Paul gives thanks for is growth in Christlikeness — the Spirit's work conforming them to Christ's character.
4: 'We ourselves boast about you in the churches of God for your steadfastness and faith in all your persecutions.' The Thessalonians' endurance under persecution mirrors the pattern of Christ's own suffering before glory (Luke 24:26; Philippians 2:8–9). Paul boasts of them as he might boast of Christ-formed suffering — their trials are not random afflictions but participation in the Christological pattern of the cross.
5: 'This is evidence of the righteous judgment of God, that you may be made worthy of the kingdom of God, for which you are also suffering.' The worthiness to enter God's kingdom is produced through suffering — a Christological axiom. Jesus taught that the Son of Man must suffer before entering glory (Luke 24:26); believers share that path. The suffering is evidence, not punishment: God's judgment is already at work in the suffering church vindicating the pattern.
6: 'Since indeed God considers it just to repay with affliction those who afflict you.' The eschatological reversal — oppressors afflicted, sufferers rest — is grounded in God's justice. But the judge is the Lord Jesus (2 Thessalonians 1:7–8). The coming judgment belongs to Christ; he executes the divine recompense. The righteous sufferers' vindication is therefore Christologically administered — Jesus as eschatological judge fulfills what YHWH promised as the avenger of the oppressed.
8: 'Inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ.' The judgment criterion is twofold: knowing God (OT covenant knowledge) and obeying the gospel of Christ. Refusal of the gospel of Jesus is grouped with refusing to know God — treating the two as equivalent. Christological: response to Jesus is equated with response to God; rejecting the Son is rejecting the Father.
9: 'They will suffer the punishment of eternal destruction, away from the presence of the Lord and from the glory of his might.' The destruction is defined spatially and relationally: exclusion from the Lord's presence. In OT terms, God's presence is blessing; exile from it is curse (Psalms 51:11; Isaiah 59:2). Applied to the Lord Jesus: the judgment of eternal destruction is exclusion from Christ's own glory. The face of Jesus is the face of God; to be excluded from it is to be excluded from God.
10: 'When he comes on that day to be glorified in his saints, and to be marveled at among all who have believed.' The parousia of Jesus is the occasion of his glorification in the saints — the saints are the medium through which his glory is displayed. This echoes Isaiah 49:3 (Israel as the locus of YHWH's glory) now Christologically applied: Jesus is glorified in his people as YHWH was glorified in Israel. The believers' transformation at his coming simultaneously manifests his glory.
11: 'To this end we always pray for you, that our God may make you worthy of his calling and may fulfill every resolve for good and every work of faith by his power.' The prayer target is Christological formation: worthiness of calling, good resolve, faithful works — all produced by God's power. The calling is the call of Christ (2 Thessalonians 1:5); the power that fulfills it is resurrection power (Philippians 3:10; Ephesians 1:19–20). The prayer is for Christ's character to be formed in them.
12: 'So that the name of our Lord Jesus may be glorified in you, and you in him, according to the grace of our God and the Lord Jesus Christ.' The mutual glorification — Christ glorified in them, they glorified in him — is the most concentrated Christological statement in the chapter. The believer's glorification is in Christ; Christ's glorification is in the believer. The mutual indwelling of John 17:21–23 is here expressed in eschatological terms: the parousia consummates a union that grace began.
